title: ServiceLayer — Services, DAOs & ModelService
description: The architecture and patterns behind Commerce Cloud's business logic layer, from DAOs to ModelService to the Facade pattern.

sections.title: The ServiceLayer Architecture
sections.content:
The ServiceLayer is the recommended architecture for all business logic in SAP Commerce Cloud. It replaces the legacy Jalo layer (which still exists underneath but should never be used directly in modern development). Understanding the layered structure is essential for the exam.

The layers from top to bottom are:

  1. Controller / Web Layer — handles HTTP requests, delegates immediately to Facades
  2. Facade Layer — orchestrates Services, converts Models to Data objects (DTOs) for the web layer
  3. Service Layer — contains business logic, operates on Models, delegates data access to DAOs
  4. DAO Layer — executes FlexibleSearch queries, returns Models
  5. ModelService / Persistence Layer — creates, saves, and removes Model objects

Each layer has a strict contract about what it can and cannot do:

  • Facades know about DTOs and Models but never write SQL
  • Services know about Models but never know about HTTP or DTOs
  • DAOs know about FlexibleSearch and Models but contain no business logic
Exam rule: Business logic belongs in Services, not Facades. Facades are orchestrators and translators. If you find complex conditional logic in a Facade, it should be refactored into a Service. The exam tests this boundary regularly through scenario questions.

sections.title: ModelService — The Persistence Gateway
sections.content:
ModelService is the central Spring bean for all persistence operations in the ServiceLayer. You never interact with the database directly — everything goes through ModelService.

Core Methods

  • modelService.create(MyModel.class) — creates a new, unsaved Model instance with defaults applied
  • modelService.save(model) — persists a single Model (INSERT or UPDATE)
  • modelService.saveAll(collection) — persists a collection of Models in one operation
  • modelService.remove(model) — deletes a Model from the database
  • modelService.refresh(model) — reloads the Model's attributes from the database, discarding unsaved changes
  • modelService.detach(model) — removes the Model from the session cache without deleting it
  • modelService.initDefaults(model) — applies default values defined in items.xml to an existing Model instance

The Session Cache

ModelService maintains a session-scoped cache of loaded Models. When you call modelService.get(pk) twice with the same PK in the same session, you get the same Java object instance. This cache is automatically flushed on save(). Understanding this is important for reasoning about dirty-checking behavior.

// Creating and saving a new product
ProductModel product = modelService.create(ProductModel.class);
product.setCode("ELEC-001");
product.setName("Laptop", Locale.ENGLISH);
modelService.save(product);

// Removing a model
ProductModel toDelete = productService.getProductForCode(catalog, "OLD-001");
modelService.remove(toDelete);
Common mistake: Calling new ProductModel() directly instead of modelService.create(ProductModel.class). The create() method registers the instance with the session and applies attribute defaults from items.xml. A manually instantiated Model bypasses both, causing subtle bugs at save time.

sections.title: Writing a Service — Interface, Implementation and Spring Wiring
sections.content:
Every Service in Commerce Cloud should follow the interface + implementation pattern. This enables the alias-based override mechanism and makes unit testing straightforward.

// 1. The interface
public interface WarrantyService {
    int getWarrantyMonths(ProductModel product);
    void extendWarranty(ProductModel product, int additionalMonths);
}

// 2. The implementation
public class DefaultWarrantyService implements WarrantyService {

    private ModelService modelService;

    @Override
    public int getWarrantyMonths(ProductModel product) {
        Integer months = product.getWarrantyMonths();
        return months != null ? months : 12; // default
    }

    @Override
    public void extendWarranty(ProductModel product, int additionalMonths) {
        int current = getWarrantyMonths(product);
        product.setWarrantyMonths(current + additionalMonths);
        modelService.save(product);
    }

    public void setModelService(ModelService modelService) {
        this.modelService = modelService;
    }
}
<!-- 3. Spring wiring -->
<alias name="defaultWarrantyService" alias="warrantyService"/>
<bean id="defaultWarrantyService"
      class="com.mycompany.DefaultWarrantyService">
  <property name="modelService" ref="modelService"/>
</bean>

Notice the pattern: default* bean ID, short alias, property injection of dependencies. This is identical to how SAP writes its own platform services, making your customizations consistent and overridable by downstream extensions.

Key principle: Services must be stateless. All state is carried in the Models they operate on or passed as method parameters. Never store a ProductModel (or any session-specific object) as a Service instance variable.

sections.title: DAOs — Data Access Objects and FlexibleSearch
sections.content:
A DAO (Data Access Object) is responsible for one thing: retrieving data from the database and returning Models. DAOs contain no business logic — they are thin wrappers around FlexibleSearch queries.

The standard base class is AbstractItemDao, which provides access to the FlexibleSearchService. Every DAO should extend this and expose specific, named query methods rather than exposing raw search capabilities to callers.

public class DefaultWarrantyDao extends AbstractItemDao
    implements WarrantyDao {

    private static final String FIND_BY_MONTHS =
        "SELECT {" + ProductModel.PK + "} "
      + "FROM {" + ProductModel._TYPECODE + "} "
      + "WHERE {" + ProductModel.WARRANTYMONTHS + "} >= ?minMonths";

    @Override
    public List<ProductModel> findProductsWithWarrantyAtLeast(int minMonths) {
        final FlexibleSearchQuery query =
            new FlexibleSearchQuery(FIND_BY_MONTHS);
        query.addQueryParameter("minMonths", minMonths);
        return getFlexibleSearchService()
            .<ProductModel>search(query)
            .getResult();
    }
}

A few important practices visible here:

  • Query strings use generated constants (ProductModel.PK, ProductModel._TYPECODE) instead of hardcoded strings, making queries refactor-safe
  • Parameters use ?paramName syntax — never string concatenation, which risks injection
  • The DAO returns List<ProductModel> — typed results, not raw objects
Exam tip: The exam distinguishes between calling FlexibleSearchService.search() (returns a SearchResult with pagination info) and FlexibleSearchService.searchUnique() (returns a single result or throws if none or multiple found). Know when to use each.

sections.title: The Facade Layer — Orchestration and DTO Conversion
sections.content:
The Facade layer sits between the web/API layer and the Service layer. Its two responsibilities are orchestration (calling multiple services and assembling a result) and translation (converting Models to DTOs via Converters).

Facades should contain no business logic. If a Facade is doing conditional calculations or data transformations beyond simple delegation, that logic belongs in a Service.

public class DefaultProductFacade implements ProductFacade {

    private ProductService productService;
    private Converter<ProductModel, ProductData> productConverter;

    @Override
    public ProductData getProductForCode(String code) {
        // 1. Delegate to Service for business logic
        ProductModel model = productService
            .getProductForCode(baseSiteService.getCurrentBaseSite()
                .getDefaultCatalog(), code);

        // 2. Convert Model to DTO for the web layer
        return productConverter.convert(model);
    }
}

Why the Facade Layer Exists

The web layer (Controllers, OCC API) must not work with Models directly because:

  • Models are tied to the Hibernate session — passing them to a serializer can trigger lazy-load exceptions
  • Models expose internal platform APIs that should not be part of a public contract
  • DTOs can be shaped specifically for the consumer (e.g. a mobile API response vs a backoffice form)
Separation of concerns summary: Controller asks Facade for data → Facade asks Service for Model → Service asks DAO for Model → DAO queries database → Model flows back up → Facade converts Model to DTO → Controller returns DTO as JSON. Each layer only knows about the layer directly below it.

sections.title: Transactions and the UserContext
sections.content:
Two cross-cutting concerns in the ServiceLayer are important for the exam: transaction management and the user context.

Transactions

Commerce Cloud wraps all ServiceLayer operations in Spring-managed transactions. By default, methods in the ServiceLayer are transactional. You can annotate your own service methods explicitly:

@Transactional
public void processOrder(OrderModel order) {
    // All changes here are atomic
    order.setStatus(OrderStatus.PROCESSING);
    modelService.save(order);
    inventoryService.reserveItems(order);
    // If inventoryService throws, the entire transaction rolls back
}

The platform uses @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRED) by default, meaning the method joins an existing transaction if one exists, or starts a new one.

The UserContext and SessionService

Many platform services behave differently depending on who the current user is. The UserService exposes getCurrentUser() which returns the active UserModel for the current thread/session.

To temporarily switch user context (e.g. in a CronJob running as admin), use the UserService:

UserModel adminUser = userService.getAdminUser();
userService.setCurrentUser(adminUser);
try {
    // Operations here run as admin
    productService.doAdminOnlyOperation();
} finally {
    userService.setCurrentUser(originalUser); // Always restore
}
Exam scenario: A CronJob fails with an access denied error on a service call. The likely cause is that CronJobs run under the anonymous user by default. The fix is to set the current user to an appropriate user (usually admin) at the start of the CronJob's perform() method, and restore it in a finally block.

questions.text: In the ServiceLayer architecture, which layer is responsible for converting Models into DTOs before returning data to the web layer?
options: Service layer — it returns DTOs directly to avoid exposing Models
DAO layer — it converts results before returning them from FlexibleSearch
Facade layer — it orchestrates Services and converts Models to DTOs via Converters
Controller layer — it calls ModelService directly to convert the data
questions.explanation: Option C is correct. The Facade layer is the translation boundary. It calls Services to get Models, then uses Converters/Populators to produce DTOs safe for the web layer. Services and DAOs always deal in Models, never DTOs. Controllers should never call ModelService directly.

questions.text: Calling 'new ProductModel()' is equivalent to calling 'modelService.create(ProductModel.class)' and both approaches produce an identical result.
questions.explanation: False. modelService.create() registers the instance with the session cache and applies attribute defaults defined in items.xml. Using new ProductModel() bypasses both, which can cause missing defaults and errors at save time. Always use modelService.create().

options: Saves any unsaved changes on the model to the database
Reloads the model's attributes from the database, discarding any unsaved in-memory changes
Clears the entire ModelService session cache for all loaded models
Re-applies default values from items.xml to all null attributes on the model
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. refresh() discards any in-memory changes and reloads the current database state for that model instance. It is useful when you suspect another thread or process has modified the record and you need a fresh view. It does not save changes — use save() for that.

questions.text: A DAO method should return which of the following?
options: DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) shaped for the specific caller's needs
Raw JDBC ResultSet objects for maximum performance
Model objects (e.g. ProductModel, OrderModel) retrieved via FlexibleSearch
Jalo objects, since DAOs sit closest to the persistence layer
questions.explanation: Option C is correct. DAOs return typed Model objects from FlexibleSearch queries. They never return DTOs (that is the Facade's job), never expose raw JDBC (FlexibleSearch is the query API), and never use Jalo (the legacy layer that the ServiceLayer replaces).

questions.text: FlexibleSearchService.searchUnique() throws an exception if zero results or more than one result are found for the query.
questions.explanation: True. searchUnique() expects exactly one result. It throws AmbiguousIdentifierException if multiple rows match and UnknownIdentifierException if none match. Use search() when zero or multiple results are valid outcomes.

questions.text: A CronJob's perform() method throws an AccessDeniedException when calling a platform service. What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: The CronJob is missing a @Transactional annotation — adding it grants the necessary permissions
CronJobs run as the anonymous user by default — set the current user to admin at the start of perform() and restore it in a finally block
The service has been overridden incorrectly — the alias is not pointing to the correct bean
The CronJob's Spring bean is declared in the wrong context file and cannot access the service
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. CronJobs execute under the anonymous user context by default, which lacks permissions for many platform service calls. The standard fix is to call userService.setCurrentUser(userService.getAdminUser()) at the start of perform() and restore the original user in a finally block.

questions.text: A Service implementation in the ServiceLayer is allowed to contain complex business logic, including conditional calculations and multi-step workflows.
questions.explanation: True. The Service layer is explicitly where business logic lives. Complex conditions, multi-step workflows, and domain rules all belong in Services. What does NOT belong in Services is HTTP handling, DTO conversion (Facade's job), or raw database queries (DAO's job).

questions.text: Why should the web layer (Controllers, OCC) not work directly with Model objects?
options: Models are not serializable and will cause JSON serialization to fail outright
Models are tied to the Hibernate session and expose internal platform APIs not appropriate for public contracts
Models cannot be passed as method arguments in Spring MVC controllers
Platform licensing restricts Model access to the Service layer only
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Models are session-bound objects — passing them to a serializer can trigger lazy-load exceptions. They also expose internal APIs that should not be part of a public web contract. DTOs are purpose-built for the consumer, serialization-safe, and decouple the API contract from internal data structures.

questions.text: What is the difference between modelService.save(model) and modelService.saveAll(collection)?
options: save() uses optimistic locking while saveAll() uses pessimistic locking
save() persists a single Model; saveAll() persists a collection of Models, which can be more efficient for bulk operations
saveAll() validates all models before saving any of them; save() skips validation
There is no functional difference — saveAll() simply calls save() in a loop internally
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. saveAll() is optimized for persisting multiple Models together, reducing the number of database round-trips compared to calling save() in a loop. While Option D is partially true in implementation, the platform can batch the operations, making saveAll() the correct choice for collections.

questions.text: In a FlexibleSearch query, parameters should be concatenated directly into the query string using Java string operations to improve readability.
questions.explanation: False. Parameters must always use the ?paramName placeholder syntax and be passed via query.addQueryParameter(). String concatenation is equivalent to SQL injection — it breaks with special characters and is a security risk. The platform's query parameterization also enables query plan caching.

questions.text: Which statement correctly describes the role of modelService.detach(model)?
options: It permanently deletes the model from the database
It removes the model from the session cache without deleting it from the database
It marks the model as read-only, preventing any further changes from being saved
It detaches the model from its parent type, converting it to a GenericItem
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. detach() removes the Model instance from the ModelService session cache. The database record is untouched. This is useful when you want to prevent accidental saves of a model you are using for read-only purposes, or when managing memory in large batch operations.

questions.text: A Facade is the correct place to add complex business rules such as price calculation logic and inventory validation.
questions.explanation: False. Facades are orchestrators and translators — they delegate to Services and convert results to DTOs. Business rules belong in the Service layer. Placing logic in Facades makes it untestable in isolation and violates the layered architecture contract.
